metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact `typ` value(s) the verifier must accept for access tokens (e.g., `at+JWT` per RFC 9068) and the exact value used in the AC4 negative-control fixture, so the executor makes no security-relevant design choice.
findings.finding: The `typ` claim value(s) accepted as valid for access tokens are never stated. The plan mandates a `typ`/token-class check per RFC 8725 §3.11 and AC4 requires a negative test for 'a wrong token class', but does not specify what the CORRECT `typ` value is (e.g., `at+JWT` per RFC 9068, or bare `JWT`, or provider-specific). An executor must make a security-relevant design choice without guidance, and could silently implement a weaker check.
findings.location: Security section ('typ'/token-class check) and AC4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The verifier MUST accept `typ: at+JWT` (RFC 9068 §2.1) and MUST reject tokens whose `typ` is `JWT` (ID-token shape) or absent. The AC4 negative-control token MUST carry `typ: JWT`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'warn/optionally reject' with a concrete default behavior (e.g., 'log a warning and proceed, but allow operators to set `auth_jwt_jwks_allow_private_hosts=true` to suppress the warning') so the executor makes no undirected security choice.
findings.finding: The SSRF mitigation for private/link-local JWKS hosts is specified as 'warn/optionally reject', leaving the executor to choose between two materially different security behaviors without a stated default.
findings.location: Security section, SSRF-safe JWKS fetch bullet
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'By default, log a WARNING and allow the fetch to proceed (to support on-premise/private deployments); add a config key `auth_jwt_jwks_require_https_public=true` (default false) that turns the warning into a hard startup error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs that verify (a) the verifier refuses to start / raises when given an `http://` JWKS URI and (b) the JWKS HTTP client uses a bounded timeout (observable oracle: startup exception for http://, or a configurable/hardcoded timeout constant asserted in a unit test).
findings.finding: Security section labels HTTPS-only JWKS fetch and a bounded HTTP timeout as 'Hard requirements', but no Acceptance Criterion verifies either constraint is enforced, making these described security deliverables orphans.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: … HTTPS-only, bounded timeout') vs Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add two AC bullet points: (1) 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises at construction when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` uses an `http://` scheme.' (2) 'Unit test or code review asserts the JWKS HTTP client is constructed with an explicit `timeout` ≤ N seconds (e.g. assert the PyJWKClient or underlying session has a non-None timeout).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'Hard requirements' vs 'warn/optionally reject' contradiction: either (a) make private/link-local rejection a firm hard requirement (and add an AC for it), or (b) move it to a 'Best-effort / future hardening' section so implementers know it is advisory.
findings.finding: The Security section's 'Hard requirements' label contradicts its own text for the SSRF/private-IP check: 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts' — 'optionally' weakens a stated hard requirement, creating an internal cross-section contradiction that will leave implementers uncertain whether the private-IP guard is mandatory or advisory.
findings.location: Security section — 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the bullet to either 'reject private/link-local hosts at construction (raise ConfigurationError)' — and add a matching AC — or move the note outside the 'Hard requirements' block with explicit 'advisory / not enforced by tests' wording.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach section that PyJWKClient does not natively rate-limit refetches on unknown `kid`s, and specify the custom cooldown wrapper (e.g. track `_last_refetch_time` and suppress refetch within `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds) that must be implemented around `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `PyJWKClient` provides a bounded refetch cooldown ('at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds, independent of legitimate rotation'), but PyJWKClient's built-in cache accepts a `lifespan` TTL and will re-fetch on unknown `kid` without any built-in rate-limiting. The cooldown is not a native PyJWKClient feature — it must be hand-implemented in application code (e.g. tracking last-fetch timestamp and suppressing refetch within the cooldown window). The plan does not acknowledge this gap, risking a false assumption that PyJWKClient handles this automatically.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — AC7 (key rotation + rate-limiting assertion)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt() triggers a JWKS refetch on unknown kid without rate-limiting. Wrap it with a module-level or instance-level `_last_refetch_at` timestamp; suppress the refetch (and raise) if `now - _last_refetch_at < auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown`, resetting only on a successful fetch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the expected `typ` claim value(s) the verifier accepts (e.g., `at+JWT` per RFC 9068, or a configurable `auth_jwt_typ` key), and name the accepted value explicitly in the AC negative-control description.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a `typ`/token-class check as a hard security requirement and AC negative control, but never defines what accepted `typ` value(s) the verifier expects (e.g., `at+JWT` per RFC 9068, `JWT`, provider-specific values). An executor cannot implement or test this check without inventing the acceptance predicate.
findings.location: Security section ('a `typ`/token-class check (RFC 8725 §3.11)') and AC line: 'a wrong token class (`typ`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The verifier requires `typ == "at+JWT"` (RFC 9068 §2.1) by default; optionally configurable via `auth_jwt_typ` (default `at+JWT`). Negative control: a JWT with `typ=JWT` or `typ=id_token` is rejected.'

findings.finding: The `typ` claim check (RFC 8725 §3.11) is mandated in both the What section and AC4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)'), but the plan never defines the accepted `typ` value vocabulary — i.e., which string(s) constitute a valid access-token `typ` (e.g. `at+JWT`, `JWT`, `Bearer`, case-sensitivity rules). An implementer must invent this set, and a test author must pick a value to use as the oracle for AC4's 'wrong token class' negative control.
findings.location: What section ('validates…the token class (`typ`)') and AC4 ('a wrong token class (`typ`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The `typ` check accepts `at+JWT` (RFC 9068 access token) and rejects all other values including `JWT` and `id_token`; comparison is case-insensitive per RFC 8725.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs with verifiable oracles for the two SSRF hard requirements: (a) the verifier raises at construction when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` uses an `http://` scheme, and (b) the JWKS HTTP client is initialised with an explicit bounded timeout (assert via a unit test or a constant).
findings.finding: Two declared 'Hard requirements' — HTTPS-only JWKS URI and bounded HTTP fetch timeout — have no Acceptance Criterion that verifies them, so an implementation can satisfy all ACs while omitting either control without triggering a test failure.
findings.location: Security section ('SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: … HTTPS-only, bounded timeout') vs Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add two AC bullet points: (1) 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises `ConfigurationError` at construction when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` is an `http://` URL.' (2) 'Unit test or code assertion verifies the `PyJWKClient` (or underlying HTTP session) is initialised with `timeout` ≤ 10 s (assert the value is not None/infinite).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `PyJWKClient.lifespan` only controls cache TTL and that a separate application-layer timestamp guard (last-refetch clock) is required to implement the per-unknown-`kid` cooldown; update the implementation notes accordingly.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` does NOT natively provide per-unknown-`kid` refetch rate-limiting. Its `lifespan` parameter controls cache TTL (how long a fetched JWKS is reused), but `get_signing_key_from_jwt` will attempt a refetch on *every* unknown `kid` without any cooldown between attempts. The plan presents `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` as a native client-side property ('cooled down to at most one refetch per N seconds, independent of legitimate rotation'), but this requires custom application-layer logic (e.g., a last-fetch timestamp guard). Without it, an attacker flooding random `kid`s causes one JWKS fetch per request — the DoS the plan intends to prevent.
findings.location: Security section — 'Key rotation without DoS'; AC line 7 (rate-limited refetch test)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Approach & rationale' or Security section, add: 'Because PyJWKClient does not provide a per-unknown-kid refetch rate limit, `JWKSTokenVerifier` must maintain a `_last_jwks_fetch: float` timestamp and refuse to re-fetch the JWKS more than once per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds regardless of how many unknown kids arrive in that window.'

findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` does not natively rate-limit or cool-down refetches for unknown `kid` values. The plan asserts 'refetch on unknown `kid` is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation' as a property of the chosen approach, but `PyJWKClient` unconditionally issues a new HTTP fetch for every unrecognized `kid` (it has a `lifespan` TTL for cached keys, but no cooldown or request-count cap on unknown-kid misses). Meeting AC7's observable oracle ('a flood of random `kid`s does not cause unbounded fetches — observable via the stateful JWKS fake's request count') requires custom wrapper logic the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Security section ('Key rotation without DoS') / AC line 7
findings.suggested_fix: Add an implementation note in the Approach section: 'Because PyJWKClient does not rate-limit unknown-kid refetches, JWKSTokenVerifier must maintain a `_last_refetch: float` timestamp and suppress any refetch triggered by an unknown kid within a configurable `_kid_refetch_cooldown` (default 30 s). The existing `lifespan` TTL controls legitimate cache expiry; the cooldown controls adversarial kid-flood suppression. AC7's fake request-count oracle validates this wrapper directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit startup check to `JWKSTokenVerifier.__init__` that rejects any `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` whose scheme is not `https`, and add a corresponding AC (or extend AC 5) to assert the verifier refuses to start with an `http://` JWKS URI.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'HTTPS-only' as a hard security requirement for the JWKS URI but `PyJWKClient` does not enforce this — it accepts any URL scheme (including `http://`). The enforcement must therefore be an explicit startup check in `JWKSTokenVerifier.__init__`, but the plan never says so, leaving a gap between the stated hard requirement and the named library surface.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: … HTTPS-only'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default e.g. 5 s) to the flat config keys, register it in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, and add an AC asserting that a JWKS fetch that exceeds the timeout raises within the specified bound.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'bounded timeout' for JWKS fetch in the Security section but specifies no default value, no config key, and no AC that enforces it. Without an explicit timeout, a slow or unresponsive JWKS endpoint can hold the fetch indefinitely, blocking the event loop (or thread) and making the timeout claim unverifiable.
findings.location: Security section — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch: … bounded timeout'; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers timeout)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (int, seconds, default 5) to the flat-key list alongside the other `auth_jwt_*` keys; pass it to `PyJWKClient(headers=..., timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout)`; add a unit-test AC: 'a JWKS endpoint that does not respond within `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` seconds causes the verifier to raise within that bound'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'warn/optionally reject' ambiguity for private/link-local JWKS URIs: declare an explicit enforcement posture (e.g., 'reject by default with an opt-out escape hatch for private-deployment testing') and add an AC that verifies the default behaviour.
findings.finding: The private/link-local SSRF blocking posture for JWKS fetch is undeclared — the Security section says 'warn/optionally reject' private hosts, which is materially weaker than the surrounding 'Hard requirements' framing and creates an ambiguous access posture on a boundary-crossing network path.
findings.location: Security section — 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Security section to: 'reject JWKS URIs that resolve to private/link-local/loopback ranges by default (configurable via `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true` for private-deployment testing).' Add AC: 'Unit test asserts the verifier raises when `auth_jwt_jwks_uri` resolves to a private-range IP (mock DNS or use a literal private IP in the URI), unless the escape-hatch flag is set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'warn/optionally reject' ambiguity for private/link-local JWKS hosts: specify the default behavior and, if configurable, add the config key to the flat-key list and an AC asserting the default.
findings.finding: The plan says SSRF protection includes 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`)' but never defines: (a) what triggers warn vs. reject, (b) whether there is a config key to switch behavior, or (c) what the DEFAULT is. This is a security decision rule without a decision.
findings.location: Security section: 'warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Recommend defaulting to reject (raise at construction time if `jwks_uri` resolves to a private/link-local address), configurable via `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=false`. Add an AC: 'Verifier raises at construction if `jwks_uri` targets a private/link-local address (default; configurable).'

findings.finding: The kid-flood rate-limit/cooldown state is referenced in Security and mandated as a test oracle in AC7, but the plan never specifies the numeric parameters (max refetches per window, window duration), the state's placement (per-instance vs. module-level), or its reset semantics. PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` has no built-in kid-flood protection, so the implementer must build custom counter state — yet its placement and thresholds are left entirely unspecified.
findings.location: Security section ('refetch on unknown `kid` is rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation') and AC7 ('unknown-`kid` refetch is rate-limited… observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the cooldown check-and-update is performed atomically (e.g., under an `asyncio.Lock` in async context or `threading.Lock` in sync context) so the 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' invariant holds under concurrency.
findings.finding: The refetch cooldown is a shared mutable timestamp that multiple concurrent coroutines/threads can read-then-write without any specified atomicity guarantee. Two concurrent requests arriving with unknown `kid`s simultaneously can both pass the 'has cooldown elapsed?' check before either updates the timestamp, resulting in two simultaneous JWKS fetches — violating the plan's own 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' invariant.
findings.location: Security section — 'at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds'; no atomicity mechanism specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The cooldown state (`last_refetch_time`) is protected by an `asyncio.Lock` (async path) or `threading.Lock` (sync path); the check-and-set is performed inside the lock to prevent concurrent refetches.' Add a unit-test AC: 'two concurrent verification requests with unknown kids trigger at most one JWKS fetch'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the parsing rule for both `scope` (space-delimited string) and `scp` (JSON array OR space-delimited, whichever is present) and the precedence when both claims appear in the same token.
findings.finding: The plan says the verifier 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes' but never specifies the parsing rule for either claim: `scope` is conventionally a space-delimited string, while `scp` is provider-specific (sometimes a space-delimited string, sometimes a JSON array). Without a normalization rule, the mapping is underspecified and implementations will diverge.
findings.location: What section: 'maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Parse `scope` as a space-delimited string; parse `scp` as a JSON array if it is an array, else as a space-delimited string. Use `scope` if present, fall back to `scp`; if both present, union them.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the cooldown timestamp is stored (recommend: instance variable on `JWKSTokenVerifier`, scoped per-verifier-instance) so the rate-limit contract is unambiguous and testable.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the cooldown rate-limit ('at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds') must be enforced, and the AC tests it via request count, but the plan does not specify WHERE the last-refetch timestamp state is stored (instance variable per-verifier, class-level shared state, or module-level). This is load-bearing: a class-level counter is shared across all verifier instances; an instance-level counter is per-verifier; both affect async isolation and testability.
findings.location: Security section: 'refetch is cooled down to at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds, independent of legitimate rotation'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The cooldown last-refetch timestamp is stored as an instance variable on `JWKSTokenVerifier` (not class-level), so each configured verifier has its own independent cooldown window.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the synchronous `PyJWKClient` fetch is safely executed in an async context (e.g., `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, ...)`) so it does not block the event loop on JWKS cache misses.
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` uses `urllib` (synchronous I/O) for JWKS fetches. If the verifier runs in an async FastMCP handler, this synchronous network call will block the event loop on every cache miss or rotation refetch, stalling all concurrent request handling for the duration of the fetch.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security — 'SSRF-safe JWKS fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap all `PyJWKClient` network calls in `asyncio.run_in_executor(None, ...)` (or equivalent thread-pool offload) and document this in the plan; alternatively, adopt an async JWKS client (e.g., `python-jose` with httpx or a thin async wrapper). Add an AC asserting the verifier does not block the event loop.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the synchronous `PyJWKClient` JWKS fetch is bridged into the async FastMCP context (e.g., `await asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor(None, client.get_signing_key_from_jwt, token)`) so cache misses and rotation refetches do not block the event loop.
findings.finding: PyJWKClient uses synchronous urllib I/O for JWKS fetches; the plan places it inside the FastMCP async request path without specifying any async bridging (e.g., run_in_executor). Every JWKS cache miss or rotation-triggered refetch will block the event loop for the full network round-trip, stalling all concurrent MCP request handling.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — 'Use PyJWT's PyJWKClient (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + kid lookup + refetch-on-rotation)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'All PyJWKClient network calls MUST be wrapped in `asyncio.run_in_executor(None, ...)` (or an equivalent thread-pool offload) so they do not block the event loop. The verifier's async `verify()` method awaits the executor future.' Alternatively, replace PyJWKClient with a thin async wrapper around httpx for JWKS fetching.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan how the synchronous PyJWKClient JWKS fetch is bridged to the async FastMCP event loop (e.g., via `asyncio.to_thread` / `run_in_executor`), or justify why blocking is acceptable (e.g., the verifier runs in a thread-pool worker).
findings.finding: PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` uses synchronous `urllib`-based HTTP for all JWKS fetches. The plan integrates it on an async FastMCP request-handler path without specifying any async bridging (e.g., `asyncio.get_event_loop().run_in_executor` or an async JWKS fetch alternative). Every JWKS fetch — both initial and on key rotation — will block the event loop for the full network RTT.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section (JWKS-fetch failure raises)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `httpx` as an explicit entry in the `dev` extra in `pyproject.toml` (or confirm it is already guaranteed transitively by an explicitly declared dep in the `[mcp]` or `dev` extra) so the integration test's `ASGITransport` usage is on a stable, declared dependency.
findings.finding: The integration test AC specifies 'httpx `ASGITransport`' but `httpx` is not declared as a dependency anywhere in pyproject.toml (neither in core `dependencies`, the `[mcp]` extra, nor the `dev` extra). While httpx is available in the current venv (confirmed via resolve_symbol — likely pulled transitively by pydantic-ai or mcp), it is not a guaranteed declared dep for the test environment that will exercise this AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200 and a garbage bearer → 401'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"httpx>=0.27"` to the `dev` list in `pyproject.toml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing the rationale for the three novel security decisions introduced by this story: (1) HTTPS-only JWKS URI with private-host rejection, (2) startup rejection of symmetric algorithms alongside JWKS, and (3) the cooldown-based rate limit for unknown-kid refetches — so future maintainers can safely evolve each boundary.
findings.finding: The plan introduces several novel security-architectural decisions (SSRF-safe JWKS fetch with private-host blocking, HS256-confusion attack prevention at startup, and rate-limited unknown-kid refetch cooldown) but only plans to regenerate reference docs for config keys — no ADR or design doc is planned to capture the rationale for these decisions, making them hard to revisit safely.
findings.location: ## Security section; ## Acceptance Criteria AC2 (MCP_ENV_VARS/reference docs); ## Approach & rationale
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNNN-jwks-verifier-security-model.md` (or a section in CLAUDE.md) that records: (a) why `jku`/`x5u` from tokens is ignored (SSRF), (b) why symmetric algs are rejected at startup on the JWKS path (RS256↔HS256 confusion attack), and (c) the DoS/rotation tradeoff that drives the `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` default, cross-referenced from the Security section of this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement (at minimum: a structured log line on JWKS fetch failure, cache hit/miss, and token rejection reason) and clarify whether the verifier should fall back to the last-cached key set on a transient refetch failure (vs. hard-fail).
findings.finding: On a transient JWKS fetch failure, the plan raises immediately (composite deny) with no retry, backoff, or fallback to the last-cached key set. A momentary network blip or brief JWKS endpoint outage will fail all JWKS-backed authentication requests for the duration, with no degraded-mode or observability to distinguish this from token-validity failures.
findings.location: Security section — 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny)'; no observability or retry/backoff mentioned anywhere
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: (1) log at WARN level on JWKS fetch failure with the URI and error; (2) on a *refetch* failure (not the initial startup fetch), optionally fall back to the previously-cached key set and log at WARN — the plan should explicitly choose one behavior; (3) add an AC: 'a JWKS fetch failure produces a structured log entry containing the JWKS URI and error type'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `auth_jwt_algorithms`, `auth_jwt_leeway`, and `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` must be operator-configurable env vars (vs. module constants) before publishing them in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`; if operator override is genuinely needed, add a bounded validation step (e.g. reject leeway > 300s, reject any symmetric algorithm) at startup.
findings.finding: The plan introduces 5 new flat env-var config keys into MCP_ENV_VARS (auth_jwt_jwks_uri, auth_jwt_issuer, auth_jwt_algorithms, auth_jwt_leeway, auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown), doubling the existing 4-entry MCP_ENV_VARS in a single story and publishing all five as permanent public API surface in server.json and the reference docs. Three of these keys (auth_jwt_algorithms, auth_jwt_leeway, auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown) have sensible, stated defaults (RS256,ES256 / 60 / 30) that are correct for virtually all OIDC deployments. The plan does not justify why these three tuning knobs need to be operator-configurable env vars vs. baked constants, which is the config-surface-proliferation question.
findings.location: What section / 'flat config keys it registers' / AC line 2 ('auth_jwt_* flat keys are registered in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the rationale: remove the 'already a transitive of `mcp`' claim and state plainly that `pyjwt[crypto]` is being added as an EXPLICIT dependency to the `[mcp]` extra (which the AC already requires) — the explicit pin is the correctness guarantee, not transitive availability.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`' as an established codebase fact justifying why `jwt` works without an explicit dep, but `pyjwt` appears nowhere in pyproject.toml (verified by exhaustive search), and the plan itself simultaneously adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra — implying it is NOT yet an explicit dependency. The 'already a transitive' claim is unverified: transitive availability is environment-specific and not guaranteed across Python versions or future mcp releases.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit' to 'Add `pyjwt[crypto]` as an explicit dependency of the `[mcp]` extra; the `[crypto]` variant pulls the `cryptography` backend needed for RS256/ES256 key operations.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact module path and name of the FastMCP JWT verification hook used as the design reference (e.g. via `python -c 'import mcp; help(mcp)'` or SDK source), and update the rationale to the confirmed symbol/path, or remove the reference if no such hook exists.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses' — naming a specific FastMCP symbol (`JWTVerifier`) as a design reference. `resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp')` and `resolve_symbol('JWTVerifier', 'mcp')` both return UNRESOLVED in the installed mcp>=1.9 environment. If this symbol does not exist, the plan's design analogy is unbacked and the implementer has no reference shape to follow.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` as a consumer in the plan (or add an AC line requiring `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to be run and its output committed before `make test` can pass).
findings.finding: tests/unit/test_gen_mcp_reference.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree is an unenumerated consumer: it diffs the committed docs/mcp-reference.md against the live generator output and will fail in CI if the regenerated reference doc is not committed before the test suite runs. The plan says 'regenerates the reference docs' in the Scope paragraph but does not enumerate this test as a consumer that must pass, nor does any AC line call it out explicitly.
findings.location: Scope section ('regenerates the reference docs') / AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC line 2: 'and `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is run and its output committed (verified by test_gen_mcp_reference.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree passing in make test).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `httpx` must be added explicitly to `[dev]` (or a test-only marker) for the integration test, or confirm the transitive dependency is acceptable and documented as a known reliance.
findings.finding: The integration test (AC line 8) uses httpx.ASGITransport, but `httpx` is not declared as a direct dependency in any install extra (pyproject.toml [mcp] or [dev]). It is currently only present as a transitive of pydantic-ai-slim. The plan does not enumerate whether `httpx` must be added to [dev] or [mcp] or whether the integration test can safely rely on the transitive.
findings.location: AC line 8 ('Integration test (httpx ASGITransport)') / pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an atomicity mechanism (e.g., an `asyncio.Lock` wrapping the cooldown check-and-fetch, or a single-flight `asyncio.Event` so concurrent waiters join the in-flight fetch rather than each launching their own) to enforce the 'at most one refetch per cooldown window' security bound.
findings.finding: The per-request unknown-kid cooldown mechanism ("at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds") is a check-then-act on a shared timestamp, which is a TOCTOU race in a concurrent async context. Multiple concurrent requests with distinct unknown kid values can simultaneously read the timestamp, all find the cooldown elapsed, and all trigger a JWKS fetch — defeating the DoS bound the mechanism is designed to enforce.
findings.location: Security section — 'refetch on an unknown kid is cooled down to at most one refetch per auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown seconds (default 30), independent of legitimate rotation, so an attacker flooding random kids cannot force more than one JWKS fetch per cooldown window'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The cooldown check-and-refetch MUST be serialized under an `asyncio.Lock` (or equivalent single-flight primitive): acquire the lock, re-check the cooldown (double-checked locking), fetch if needed, update the timestamp, release. Concurrent unknown-kid requests arriving during an in-flight refetch MUST await the same fetch result rather than each triggering their own.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the actual FastMCP auth extension point (correct module path and interface for any `JWTVerifier` or auth-hook the MCP SDK provides) before committing to the implementation approach, to confirm alignment or deliberate deviation.
findings.finding: The plan justifies using PyJWT's JWKS path by stating it is 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses', implying FastMCP ships a JWTVerifier the implementation should align with. However, `JWTVerifier` is unresolved in the installed MCP SDK, and the FastMCP module path is also unresolved. If FastMCP's JWTVerifier does not exist (or lives at a different import path), the plan's architectural rationale at the SDK seam is unverified and the implementation may diverge from (or conflict with) any actual SDK auth abstractions.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section — 'the same shape FastMCP's JWTVerifier uses'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline the `AccessToken` field schema (at minimum: `scopes`, `expires_at`, `resource`, and their types) in this plan, or add a hard prerequisite that S2 publishes a stable, importable `AccessToken` type before S3 implementation begins.
findings.finding: The plan says the verifier 'stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url`' on the returned `AccessToken` and sets `expires_at` from `exp`, referencing an `AccessToken` type that comes from S2. The `AccessToken` schema (its fields, types, and the `resource` field in particular) is not defined in this plan and is absent from the codebase, leaving the return-type contract undefined for an S3 implementor.
findings.location: What section and AC: 'stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url`', 'returned `AccessToken` fields'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specification for the 'HTTP 200 but malformed/empty JWKS body' case, clarifying whether it should raise (like a fetch failure) or return None (like a definitively invalid token), and add a corresponding AC or note.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that 'a JWKS-fetch failure raises' and 'a definitively invalid token returns None', but leaves a third case unaddressed: the JWKS fetch succeeds (HTTP 200) but the response body is malformed, empty, or contains no keys matching any algorithm. No fallback or error path is specified for this intermediate failure mode.
findings.location: Security section: 'A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny); a definitively invalid token returns None'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A malformed or empty JWKS body (HTTP 200 but unparseable or zero keys) is treated as a fetch failure and raises, so the composite denies the request.'

findings.finding: The rate-limit/cooldown value for unknown-kid flood defense is unspecified — the plan says 'rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation' and AC7 says 'unbounded fetches' must not occur, but gives no concrete threshold (e.g., max 1 refetch per 30 s, or at most N total refetches for M distinct kids). The executor must choose a number, and the test assertion cannot be written as a falsifiable inequality without one.
findings.location: Security section ('rate-limited/cooled-down'); AC7 ('unknown-kid refetch is rate-limited … observable oracle via request count')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'Unknown-kid refetch is cooled down to at most 1 refetch per 30 s (configurable via `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown`, default 30).' Update AC7: 'assert the JWKS fake receives ≤ 2 GET requests when the verifier is called 50 times in rapid succession with random kids (oracle: `fake.request_count <= 2`).

findings.finding: The rate-limit policy for unknown-`kid` JWKS refetch has no concrete bound stated anywhere in the plan. AC7 asserts 'unbounded fetches do not occur' with 'observable oracle via request count,' but without a numeric threshold (e.g., 'at most 1 refetch per 30 s' or 'max 2 fetches for a flood of 50 requests') the oracle is not falsifiable — the test writer must invent the threshold and any non-infinite count satisfies 'not unbounded.'
findings.location: Security section ('rate-limited/cooled-down independently of legitimate rotation'); AC7 ('unknown-kid refetch is rate-limited … observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, replace 'rate-limited/cooled-down' with: 'at most 1 JWKS refetch per 30 s for unrecognized kids (cooldown timer resets on a successful rotation refetch); a flood of N distinct unknown kids in < 30 s triggers exactly 1 refetch.' In AC7, change the oracle to: 'assert the fake received ≤ 2 total requests (1 initial load + 1 rotation refetch) for a flood of 50 random-kid tokens within a 1 s window.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
